steps.title: Recognize that backsolving is efficient for simple integer choices
steps.content: The answer choices are simple integers, so we can test each value directly in the equation $(x + 2)(x + 3) = (x - 2)(x - 3) + 10$ rather than solving algebraically.

steps.title: Test the choices
steps.content:
Backsolving — testing choice B (0):
Left: $(0+2)(0+3) = 2 \times 3 = 6$
Right: $(0-2)(0-3)+10 = 6+10 = 16$
Since $6 \neq 16$, B fails.

Testing choice C (-2):
Left: $(-2+2)(-2+3) = 0 \times 1 = 0$
Right: $(-2-2)(-2-3)+10 = 20+10 = 30$
Since $0 \neq 30$, C fails.

Testing choice A (1):
Left: $(1+2)(1+3) = 3 \times 4 = 12$
Right: $(1-2)(1-3)+10 = 2+10 = 12$
Since $12 = 12$, choice A works. That matches choice A — A is correct.
